metadata.description: Essential Chinese vocabulary for weather, seasons, and describing experiences

content.name: so_such
content.side_f: Why is it so hot today?
content.side_g: 那么 modifies adjectives/verbs to mean 'so/that (much).' Often paired with 怎么 for surprise ('怎么那么...?' = 'how come so...?'). Contrast with 这么 (zhème, 'this'): 那么 points to a degree already known or assumed; 这么 points to what's right here. Also a discourse connector ('then, in that case').

content.name: fun
content.side_e: Zhèige gōngyuán hěn hǎowánr, wǒmen chángcháng qù.
content.side_f: This park is fun; we often go. (lit. 'good-play')
content.side_g: 好玩儿 is colloquial Beijing speech with the 儿化 suffix; describes places, activities, toys, people. Not used for jokes/movies (use 有意思/好笑). Typical collocates: 好玩儿的地方, 真好玩儿. Southern speakers often drop the 儿.

content.name: go_out
content.side_e: Wàitou xià yǔ, wǒ bù xiǎng chū qu.
content.side_f: It's raining outside; I don't want to go out.
content.side_g: 出去 is a directional complement: 出 (exit) + 去 (away from speaker). Opposite of 出来. Object inserts between: 出去玩儿, 出门去, or split 出学校去. Neutral-tone 去 in spoken use. Contrast 出去 (leaving to elsewhere) with 回去 (returning).

content.name: very
content.side_g: 非常 is a high-degree adverb, stronger than 很 and more formal than 特别. Modifies adjectives and psych-verbs (非常喜欢) but not action verbs directly. Common in written/formal speech; spoken register favors 特别 or 真. Can stack: 非常非常 for emphasis.

content.name: terrible
content.side_a: In a terrible mess; how terrible
content.side_e: Zāogāo, wǒ wàng le dài sǎn!
content.side_f: How terrible — I forgot to bring an umbrella! (lit. 'rotten-cake')
content.side_g: 糟糕 is an exclamation of dismay or an adjective ('awful, a mess'). Functions like English 'oh no' / 'shoot.' Collocates: 真糟糕, 糟糕透了 (utterly awful), 情况很糟糕. Informal but not vulgar — safe in any casual context.

content.name: rain
content.side_e: Jīntiān yòu xià yǔ le, zhēn zāogāo.
content.side_f: It's raining again today — how awful.
content.side_g: 下雨 is a verb-object compound: 下 (fall) + 雨 (rain). Insert modifiers between: 下大雨, 下了一场雨. Same pattern builds 下雪 (snow). Use 在下雨 for ongoing, 下雨了 for onset. Subject is usually omitted — weather is impersonal.

content.name: again
content.side_e: Zuótiān xià yǔ, jīntiān yòu xià yǔ.
content.side_f: It rained yesterday, and it's raining again today.
content.side_g: 又 marks a repetition that has already happened or is happening — past/present. Contrast 再 (zài), which marks a repetition that will happen. So 又下雨了 (rained again, realized) vs 再下一次 (rain one more time, future). Often pairs with 了: 又...了.

content.name: interview
content.side_e: Míngtiān wǒ yǒu yí ge hěn zhòngyào de miànshì.
content.side_f: Tomorrow I have a very important interview.
content.side_g: 面试 works as both verb ('to interview') and noun ('an interview'), usually for jobs or school admissions. Collocates: 参加面试, 准备面试, 面试官 (interviewer). Contrast with 采访 (cǎifǎng, journalistic interview). Measure word: 个 or 次.

content.name: go_back
content.side_e: Tài wǎn le, wǒ děi huí qu le.
content.side_f: It's too late; I have to go back now.
content.side_g: 回去 = 回 (return) + 去 (away from speaker). Use 回去 when speaking from a location you're leaving; use 回来 when the destination is the speaker's location. Object inserts: 回家去, 回宿舍去. Often sentence-final with 了 to mark imminent departure.

content.name: winter
content.side_e: Běijīng de dōngtiān fēicháng lěng, chángcháng xià xuě.
content.side_f: Winters in Beijing are extremely cold; it often snows.
content.side_g: 冬天 is the everyday word for winter; the more literary 冬季 (dōngjì) appears in forecasts and formal writing. No measure word needed after 在 or 的: 在冬天, 冬天的时候. Common collocates: 过冬 (spend winter), 冬天来了.

content.name: summer
content.side_e: Jiāzhōu de xiàtiān bù tài rè, hěn shūfu.
content.side_f: California summers aren't too hot; they're very comfortable.
content.side_g: 夏天 is the colloquial word for summer, formal equivalent 夏季. Pattern 夏天的时候 = 'in summer.' Pairs naturally with 热, 热闹, 放假. Weather sentences often drop the subject: 夏天热 is a complete, idiomatic sentence.

content.name: hot
content.side_e: Jīntiān tài rè le, wǒ bù xiǎng chū qu.
content.side_f: It's too hot today; I don't want to go out.
content.side_g: 热 describes temperature (天气热), food (热菜), and metaphorically popularity (热门). Predicate-adjective use does not take 是: say 今天很热, not 今天是热. Intensifiers: 很热, 非常热, 热死了 (hot to death = unbearably hot).

content.name: spring
content.side_e: Chūntiān de shíhou, huār dōu kāi le.
content.side_f: In spring, all the flowers bloom.
content.side_g: 春天 evokes renewal; formal 春季. Common set phrases: 春天来了 (spring has come), 春暖花开 (warm spring, blooming flowers — a chengyu). Pairs with 暖和 (nuǎnhuo, warm). Culturally tied to 春节 (Spring Festival / Lunar New Year).

content.name: autumn
content.side_e: Qiūtiān bù lěng yě bú rè, fēicháng shūfu.
content.side_f: Autumn is neither cold nor hot; it's extremely comfortable.
content.side_g: 秋天 = autumn/fall; formal 秋季. Often described with 凉快 (liángkuai, cool-pleasant) and 舒服. Cultural resonances: 中秋节 (Mid-Autumn Festival), 秋高气爽 (crisp autumn air — chengyu). Pattern 不...也不... expresses balanced negation.

content.name: comfortable
content.side_e: Jiāzhōu de tiānqì hěn shūfu, bù lěng yě bú rè.
content.side_f: California's weather is very comfortable — not cold, not hot.
content.side_g: 舒服 covers physical comfort (bed, clothes, weather) and bodily well-being: 不舒服 is the standard way to say 'feel unwell/sick.' Reduplication 舒舒服服(地) intensifies as an adverb. Contrast 舒适 (shūshì), which is more written/formal.

content.name: california
content.side_e: Wǒ jiějie zhù zài Jiāzhōu, nàr de chūntiān fēicháng hǎo.
content.side_f: My older sister lives in California; spring there is wonderful.
content.side_g: 加州 is the short form of 加利福尼亚州 (Jiālìfúníyà zhōu). 州 = U.S.-style state; Chinese provinces take 省. Used without 的 before place suffix: 加州大学, 加州人.
